text: Journal of Geophysical Research: Atmospheres. Elemental composition of OA (EOA) and related quantities such as the oxidation state of carbon are important for source apportionment, OA processes, and impact studies. These quantities are commonly derived from aerosol mass spectrometer measurements using the high-resolution time-of-flight (HR-ToF) version (HR-ToF-AMS) of the Aerodyne aerosol mass spectrometer. This study extends the previous work on HR-ToF-AMS derivations of atomic oxygen-to-carbon (O : C), hydrogen-to-carbon (H : C), and organic mass-to-organic carbon (OM : OC) for laboratory generated multifunctional oxidized organic aerosol (OA) standards, in order to better test existing approaches to deriving elemental composition, sources, and related quantities from ambient measurements. Specifically, this work builds a more robust laboratory standard dataset that is used to assess the ability of different approaches to derive given standards’ actual elemental compositions (referred to as the truth). Specifically, we evaluate two AMS methods using these lab standards. The first approach, referred to as the Aiken-Explicit method, has previously taken into account the real amplitudes of all ions measured by the HR-ToF-AMS while fitting OA composition and fragmentation given an assumed composition of the standard aerosol species. The second, also known as the Aiken-Ambient method, takes into account real amplitudes of the major peaks at m/z of about 30 and 28, corresponding to the ions CO+ and H2O+, respectively. Gas phase interference at these peaks is common and has to be accounted for and was in the previous method only inferred from the fits of the assumed species given a particular assumed composition for ambient aerosol species and measured AMS spectra. We show that for our larger lab standard dataset that includes additional molecules and both high and low oxidation states the Aiken-Explicit method can reproduce known standard lab EOA values to within about ±20% for O : C and ±12% for H : C on average absolute value of relative errors. The Aiken-Ambient method also yields a generally correct depiction of lab EOA standard values but to a lesser degree with biases of up to ±28% for O : C (vs. ±20% for Aiken-Explicit) and up to ±14% for H : C (vs. ±12% for Aiken-Explicit) although generally in the low bias direction. The low bias is more pronounced for alcohols and simple diacids. Using detailed investigation of the high-resolution mass spectra for all the lab standards measured with these AMS methods, we derive several qualitative and quantitative conclusions regarding the measurement and fitting of ions derived from specific primary or secondary functional groups associated with multifunctional oxidized species of various composition. We utilize information obtained about the measurement of fragmentation processes in the vaporizer of the HR-ToF-AMS which derives AMS-VUV (combined space and time resolved vacuum ultraviolet photoionization) information. AMS-VUV observations of fragmentation (chemical alteration) of the lab standards when measured by the HR-ToF and detection on the vaporizer itself and derived ions, indicates that hydration and decarboxylation take place, specifically dehydration, even on relatively low vaporizer temperatures of about 200 °C and very commonly after warming to a temperature of about 600 °C. Thus, a more sophisticated approach incorporating factors such as thermal stability that possibly include dehydration and decarboxylation that take place while on the warm sampling nozzle and prior to or even while being detected by the VUV source, has been formulated which better simulates real aerosol ambients and has been termed here as the Improved-Ambient (IA) method. When utilizing all of the lab data and analyses and conclusions above, the (IA) approach is able to improve (i.e., decrease the bias in the derived) values associated with any lab standard and which typically differ from the known lab values by less than ±28% (and an average absolute value relative to known lab values of about 24% for O : C or by about ±13% for lab EOA standards when averaging the absolute value relative bias. For laboratory mixes that closely represent a plausible description of the complex mixture of oxidized OA that may be present in field atmospheres, the differences for any given lab standard between the two AMS derived elemental composition approaches were very much smaller than their corresponding errors relative to the known lab values and this improves the estimated AMS accuracy that describes such complex ambient mixes when calculated relative to the known lab EOA standards. Using the new and better representation of lab OA (species) standards with their known actual values and improved (IA) procedure of modeling the fit for elemental ratios of ambient OA spectra, it is determined that elemental composition and OS C for ambient OA calculated from these new procedures are higher than the corresponding Aiken-Ambient approach with OA values for the three derived elemental composition metrics of O : C, H : C, and OM : OC having median ratios relative to Aiken-Ambient of 1.27 (7.69), 1.11 (5.59), and 1.09 (5.85), respectively. Thus there is a higher relative oxygen content for the corresponding OA ambients using this approach as well as possible improved representativeness for the complex and real world ambient OA. Lastly the (carbon) OS C AMS-derived values for ambient OA are virtually unchanged, having an average relative error compared to the new ambient Aiken-Explicit-derived values of about 6% which suggests relative immunity from contamination by water during collection or in the AMS or by any associated secondary thermal reactions during this same period while the signal corresponding to that aerosol particle is being generated on the vaporizer of the detection part of the instrument, suggesting greater robustness for OS C compared to O : C for analysis and source determination of aerosols as indicated in the Summary and Conclusion.
explanation: High technical detail and coherent structure suggest human authorship; AI could imitate this, but the nuance and integrated domain knowledge make it more likely to be written by a human.
